title: Supervisory Stress Test Documentation Final 2026 Global Market Shock Component
summary: The Federal Reserve Board released the final documentation for the 2026 Global Market Shock (GMS) component of the Supervisory Stress Test, effective February 2026. The GMS applies instantaneous hypothetical shocks to financial risk factors (equity returns, FX, interest rates, volatilities, commodities, credit spreads) to assess capital resilience of trading books under severely adverse market conditions. Firms with trading assets/liabilities of $50 billion or more, or trading activity equal to 10% or more of total consolidated assets, must incorporate these shocks.
key_points: GMS applies to Q1 of the stress test projection horizon; losses are recognized instantaneously with no subsequent changes
Firms subject to GMS: those with $50 billion+ in aggregate trading assets/liabilities, or trading ≥10% of total consolidated assets, excluding Category IV firms under tailoring framework
2025 stress test as-of date was October 11, 2024; as-of date changes annually and does not coincide with year-end
Risk factors include: public equity returns, exchange rates, government yields (e.g., 10-year Treasuries), implied volatilities, commodity futures, credit spreads/prices
Losses are measured using mark-to-market accounting under U.S. GAAP, unlike macroeconomic scenarios which use accrual accounting
GMS is explicitly not a forecast; it is a hypothetical scenario for assessing capital strength under financial distress
why_it_matters: This documentation establishes the regulatory methodology for how the Fed stress-tests trading book risks across large banks. The GMS design determines which market moves banks must survive, affecting capital planning, risk limits, and systemic risk assessment. Market participants monitoring bank resilience and regulatory capital requirements need to understand the shock magnitude and factor relationships defined here.
market_structure_context: The GMS defines a standardized instantaneous shock applied to trading positions as of a specific as-of date. This affects how banks mark their trading books, measure counterparty credit exposure, and maintain capital ratios. The methodology includes correlation structures between primary, secondary, and remaining risk factors, which determines how shocks propagate across asset classes in the stress scenario.
important_numbers: 10% trading assets threshold relative to total consolidated assets
9-quarter projection horizon for macroeconomic scenario (GMS applies to Q1 only)
